item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I forgot to attribute the text used in the engraving section. The original translation was taken from Wikipedia, via Patricia Utechin. The middle English text, before being altered and the sort, is from here.

32] Bertram, Jerome (2003). "Medieval Inscriptions in Oxfordshire" (PDF). Oxoniensia. LXVVIII: 30. ISSN 0308-5562.

Utechin, Patricia (1990) [1980]. Epitaphs from Oxfordshire (2nd ed.). Oxford: Robert Dugdale. p. 39. ISBN 978-0-946976-04-1.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much, Ellie, really appreciate it. Will be more mindful in the future.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Note that the third image is from here and it licensed under CC BY-SA 2.0.

item.title: A special Thank You
item.content:encoded:
Hey Guys! New update, aa it were. Thanks to the amazing Oricat, this skip got a fresh new coat of paint, with errors all hone and a much cleaner structure.

Can't thank them enough!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much. The repetition was done on purpose; my grandmother had dementia and had difficult renembering things, and had to have it repeated to her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. While I agree with what Roget said, especially in terms of redundancy in the logs I can't help but recontextualize the text through your memory of your late grandmother. I know there should be a separation between author and article, but reading your comment at the end of this long narrative really made me think on it long after reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I like the premise but I feel like the logs are mostly repeating the same idea of regret over and over again without really expounding on it in a meaningful way. The first test log was interesting but the interviews only restated information already present in the description and the last notes felt flat. I think cutting everything after the testing log and expanding on those interactions would be much more enjoyable. I think there’s a lot more room in that space than we see here.

I also fixed a few formatting errors. Overall it’s a downvote from me right now but there’s potential here.

Oh also the redaction in the containment procedures spoils what are otherwise well thought-out and original procedures.

item.title: Photo Credit.
item.content:encoded:
All photos from wikipedia.

First photo from here: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dead_bell

Second Photo from here: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost

And the third from here: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taynuilt Image may have been deleted from page…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will Lis, I will visit your funeral with a dog and a flower. I know Lis, how beautiful is a funeral? -Fernando Arrabel, Fando and Lis. (1966)

Firstly, this skip was written in memory of my late grandmother, Lucy. She was a Catholic, and a lover of mysteries and the supernatural, and it only felt fitting to dedicate her.

Special thanks to the lovely Corpse of Bixby and Shanor for Greenlighting, Oricat and Oniman7 for drafting. Thank you to N_Aepic_Fael and Mayoculpa for feedback. Thanks are also in order to Roget and pxdnbluesoul for fixing errors I missed.

This is my first skip, and I greatly appreciate you for checking this out.
